question: What is software architecture?
shortAnswer: Software architecture is the set of structures, boundaries, relationships, and guiding decisions that let a team reason about a system and its quality attributes. Architectural significance is contextual: a decision matters when changing it is costly or affects important qualities. Architecture is not finished before coding; it is continuously tested and evolved as the system and its constraints change.
detailedAnswer:
Software architecture is more than a diagram or a technology list. It describes the structures used to build and operate a system, the responsibilities and relationships of its parts, and the principles that constrain their evolution.

A decision is architecturally significant when it materially affects qualities such as availability, security, performance, deployability, or cost of change. Significance depends on context: a database choice may be difficult to reverse in one system and replaceable behind a well-tested boundary in another.

Architecture also includes feedback. Fitness functions, SLOs, dependency rules, delivery metrics, and incident learning test whether an intended architecture still works in practice. Emergent discoveries can require an ADR or a boundary change after implementation begins.

In SGN3, examples include retaining a monolithic deployment for local transactions, using package/service rules to limit coupling, and using durable messaging for work that should not block a user request. Database schemas support organization, but they are evidence of a module boundary only when ownership and dependency rules are also enforced.
keyPoints: Structures, relationships, boundaries, and guiding decisions
Structures for reasoning about the system
Not a one-time activity — architecture evolves
Prioritization of quality attributes (what matters MOST)
Architecture is validated and evolved through operational and delivery feedback
redFlags: "Architecture is the diagram we draw at the beginning"
Confusing architecture with technology stack
"We don't have an architecture" (every system has one, even if implicit)
Cannot name an architectural decision in their own system

followUps.question: What is the difference between architecture and design?
followUps.hint: The boundary is contextual; discuss scope, quality impact, stakeholders, and cost of change instead of a rigid taxonomy.

followUps.question: Who is responsible for architecture in your team?
followUps.hint: Everyone contributes, but someone must own the vision and ensure decisions are consistent.

realExperience.title: SGN3 architectural boundaries in practice
realExperience.description: Monolithic deployment, service/package dependency rules, database organization, and durable messaging are long-lived decisions in SGN3. Schemas help organize ownership but do not create boundaries by themselves; conventions, review, and architecture tests provide the enforcement.

question: Which quality attributes do you consider before selecting an architecture?
shortAnswer: I start with measurable business scenarios, then consider reliability and availability, security and privacy, latency and throughput, scalability, recoverability, modifiability, testability, deployability, observability, and cost. It is not a fixed checklist: I rank the qualities that matter for this system, state the trade-offs, and define evidence that will show whether the design meets them.
detailedAnswer:
Quality attributes drive architecture only when they are concrete and measurable. Instead of saying a system must be fast and available, define scenarios: the source of a request, expected workload, normal and failure conditions, the measured response, and the target response.

Common attributes include availability, reliability, latency, throughput, scalability, security, recoverability, maintainability, testability, deployability, observability, accessibility, and cost. Not every attribute has the same priority in every part of a system.

Trade-offs must also be stated precisely:
- More redundancy can improve availability but increases cost and operational complexity.
- Aggressive caching can reduce latency but creates invalidation and staleness concerns.
- Independent services can improve deployment autonomy while making consistency and diagnosis harder.
- Under a network partition, a distributed system may have to reject some operations or accept divergent data; CAP is not a general choice between consistency and availability during normal operation.

The output should be a small set of ranked, measurable scenarios and the architectural tactics intended to satisfy them. Those targets are then verified with tests and production telemetry.
keyPoints: You cannot optimize ALL quality attributes — trade-offs required
Availability, scalability, performance, maintainability, testability, deployability
Quality attributes DRIVE architectural decisions (not technologies)
Different systems prioritize differently (startup vs enterprise vs real-time)
Measure and set targets (SLOs) not just aspirational goals
redFlags: "We optimize everything" (impossible — trade-offs exist)
Choosing technology before understanding quality needs
Cannot name the trade-offs in their own architecture
Only thinking about functional requirements

followUps.question: How do you measure if your architecture achieves a quality attribute?
followUps.hint: Fitness functions, SLOs, metrics. Architecture evaluation is ongoing, not one-time.

followUps.question: Which quality-attribute trade-off did SGN3 make and how would you verify it?
followUps.hint: Avoid claiming that monoliths cannot scale horizontally; discuss actual coupling, deployment, transaction, capacity, and operational evidence.

question: How do you decide between a monolith and microservices?
shortAnswer: I would compare the required boundaries and quality attributes: ownership, release autonomy, consistency, failure isolation, scaling profile, and operational capability. A modular monolith is often the lower-risk starting point because it keeps calls and transactions local, but it still needs enforced internal boundaries. Services become attractive when independent ownership or operation delivers measurable value that outweighs network, data-consistency, and platform costs.
detailedAnswer:
I start with the forces, not a preferred style. A single deployable is attractive when local transactions, simple delivery, and low operational overhead matter. It can still scale horizontally when its state and infrastructure permit it. Its main risks are usually coupling, coordinated releases, and poorly enforced ownership—not the deployment shape alone.

Services are useful when a boundary needs genuinely independent ownership, release cadence, fault isolation, technology, or scaling. Those benefits have a price: remote calls, partial failures, duplicated platform work, harder observability, and explicit cross-service consistency. More teams do not automatically imply more services; Conway's Law is a pressure to examine, not a sizing formula.

A modular monolith is a deliberate option, not merely a temporary stage. Modules should own behavior and data access, expose small contracts, and have dependency rules verified by tests or tooling. A database schema can aid organization, but it does not enforce a module boundary by itself.

For an existing system, I would measure the current pain and extract only a boundary whose autonomy benefit exceeds the distribution cost. I would also define success criteria—for example release lead time, failure containment, or resource isolation—before changing the topology.
keyPoints: Choose from context and measurable forces, not a fixed team-size rule
Team topology, domain coupling, and operational capability are design forces
A modular monolith still requires real dependency and ownership boundaries
Microservices add distributed complexity (network, consistency, debugging)
Conway's Law: architecture follows communication structure
redFlags: "Microservices are always better" (cargo culting)
"We need microservices for scalability" without measuring current limits
Not considering operational maturity required
Choosing architecture based on resume, not problem

followUps.question: What is a modular monolith and how does it help?
followUps.hint: Describe independently understandable modules inside one deployable, plus concrete ownership and dependency enforcement.

followUps.question: How does Conway's Law influence this decision?
followUps.hint: Treat communication paths as a design force. Team topology may reveal useful boundaries, but it does not mechanically determine service count.

clarifyingQuestions: Do different parts need independent deployment?
What is the operational maturity (monitoring, incident response)?
Is this greenfield or evolving an existing system?

realExperience.title: SGN3: modular monolith by design
realExperience.description: SGN3 keeps closely related enrollment, scheduling, and financial workflows in one deployment, while service-layer contracts and architecture rules limit dependency direction. Schemas aid organization but are not the enforcement mechanism on their own. An integration-facing satellite service illustrates selective extraction where a separate lifecycle has concrete value.

question: Explain Hexagonal Architecture. How does it help a Jakarta EE application?
shortAnswer: Hexagonal Architecture puts application behavior behind ports and connects delivery or infrastructure concerns through adapters. An inbound adapter calls a use-case port; the application calls outbound ports for capabilities such as persistence or messaging. The important property is dependency direction: infrastructure depends on application contracts. In Jakarta EE, REST resources, JSF beans, JPA repositories, and JMS clients can be adapters around a framework-light core.
detailedAnswer:
Ports and Adapters organizes the system around application behavior rather than frameworks.

- Inbound ports express use cases the application offers. REST resources, JSF beans, scheduled jobs, and message consumers are inbound adapters that translate external input into those calls.
- Outbound ports express capabilities a use case needs, such as loading a student, publishing an event, or consulting an external system. JPA repositories, JMS producers, and REST clients implement those contracts.
- Domain objects and application services remain unaware of HTTP, JSF, SQL, or broker details wherever that separation pays off.

An interface is not automatically a port. It should represent an application boundary and be owned from the application's perspective, rather than mirror every infrastructure class. Dependency inversion also does not make adapters instantly interchangeable: persistence semantics, transaction boundaries, and query capabilities can still leak through a poor contract. The transaction boundary normally wraps the application use case or an inbound adapter; the snippet below focuses on dependency direction and deliberately omits the Jakarta EE wiring and transaction interceptor.

For a Jakarta EE system, this architecture enables fast use-case tests with fakes, keeps delivery concerns out of business rules, and creates seams for incremental modernization. It should be applied selectively: straightforward CRUD may need only clear layers, while complex rules and volatile integrations benefit most.
keyPoints: Ports express application boundaries, not arbitrary interfaces
Inbound adapters invoke use cases; outbound adapters implement needed capabilities
Infrastructure dependencies point toward application-owned contracts
Transaction and persistence semantics still require deliberate boundaries
Use the pattern where isolation and testability justify its abstraction cost
redFlags: Confusing hexagonal with layered architecture
"It's just interfaces" (misses the dependency direction principle)
Cannot explain the benefit for testing
Over-engineering simple CRUD with hexagonal (YAGNI)

followUps.question: What is the difference between Clean Architecture and Hexagonal?
followUps.hint: Same principle (dependency inversion). Clean adds more layers (entities, use cases, interface adapters). Hexagonal is simpler (core + ports + adapters).

followUps.question: When is Hexagonal Architecture overkill?
followUps.hint: Simple CRUD with no complex domain logic. The abstraction layers add cost without benefit.

codeExamples.title: Core and persistence adapter (transactional wiring omitted)

visualBlocks.title: Hexagonal in SGN3 terms
visualBlocks.content:
Inbound Adapters           Core              Outbound Adapters
        ───────────────           ─────             ─────────────────
        WebBean (JSF)      →    Business     →    Repository (JPA)
        REST Endpoint      →    (domain)     →    RestClient (Moodle)
        MDB (JMS)          →                 →    JMS Producer
                                   │
                           defines interfaces
                           (ports = contracts)

realExperience.title: SGN3 approximates hexagonal
realExperience.description: SGN3's WebBean→Service→Business→Repository flow provides useful seams that resemble ports and adapters, especially where business code is isolated from JSF or integration clients. Calling it an approximation is more accurate than claiming a pure hexagonal architecture: entities and transaction concerns may still carry persistence coupling, while architecture tests enforce selected dependency rules.

question: How do you define module or service boundaries?
shortAnswer: I look for cohesive business capabilities with their own language, rules, change patterns, and ownership. A bounded context is a useful candidate, not an automatic one-to-one mapping to a module or service. The boundary should own changes to its behavior and data and expose a deliberate contract. Schemas can support that ownership, but dependency rules, APIs, tests, and team practices make the boundary real.
detailedAnswer:
I discover candidate boundaries from business workflows, terminology, invariants, ownership, and what changes together. Enrollment, scheduling, finance, and certification may be separate contexts, but real use cases and transaction needs determine the final cut.

I then test each candidate:
- Can the team explain its responsibility without using another module's internal model?
- Which invariants must be consistent in one transaction?
- Is cross-boundary communication purposeful, or is every request chatty?
- Does the boundary have stable ownership and a small public contract?
- Would independent deployment, scaling, or failure isolation provide measurable value?

High communication is a diagnostic signal, not proof that the boundary is wrong. Some workflows legitimately coordinate several capabilities. The design must make that orchestration and its consistency model explicit.

Enforcement can include package visibility, architecture tests, repository ownership, API or event contracts, and review rules. A schema is useful for naming and permissions, but shared database credentials or unrestricted joins can bypass the intended boundary. I normally start with a module boundary and extract a service only when its autonomy benefit justifies distributed transactions, operations, and observability.
keyPoints: Boundaries by domain/business capability, not technical layer
Module owns: its data, its rules, its public interface
Use communication and transaction patterns to test candidate boundaries
Enforce boundaries with ownership, contracts, architecture rules, and permissions
Extract service only when coupling pain > distribution pain
redFlags: Boundaries by technical layer (all controllers, all services, all repos)
"Everything is a microservice" without domain analysis
Cannot explain how to validate a boundary
No enforcement mechanism (boundaries exist only on paper)

followUps.question: What is an anti-corruption layer and when do you need it?
followUps.hint: Translation layer between modules with different models. Prevents one module's model from infecting another.

followUps.question: How do you handle data that two modules both need?
followUps.hint: Keep one authoritative owner; consumers can call a contract or maintain purpose-specific projections. A shared kernel or database can be a conscious, governed trade-off rather than an accidental dependency.

realExperience.title: SGN3 schema organization as a boundary aid
realExperience.description: SGN3 uses multiple schemas and service-layer access to organize a large operational model. In the interview, name only the write-ownership and dependency rules you can demonstrate personally. Cross-schema reporting reads are a pragmatic exception, so schemas may support intended boundaries without proving isolation on their own.

question: How do you handle partial failures in a distributed system?
shortAnswer: I first define the operation's deadline, criticality, consistency needs, and failure semantics. Then I combine bounded timeouts, retries only for transient and safely repeatable work, circuit breaking, concurrency isolation, idempotency, and observable degradation. The objective is not to hide every failure; it is to contain it, preserve the most valuable behavior, and give operators and callers an honest outcome.
detailedAnswer:
Partial failure is expected whenever work crosses a process or network boundary. I design the response from the use case rather than apply one policy globally.

1. **Deadlines and cancellation:** Allocate the caller's total time budget across dependencies. Connect and response timeouts should be based on measured latency and the business deadline, and abandoned work should be cancelled where possible.
2. **Retries:** Retry only outcomes known to be transient and only when the operation is idempotent or protected by an idempotency key. Status codes alone are insufficient—a 409 can represent a retryable optimistic conflict, while a timeout may leave an unknown write result. Use exponential backoff, jitter, a retry budget, and respect server guidance.
3. **Containment:** Circuit breakers can stop repeatedly calling an unhealthy dependency. Bulkheads, concurrency limits, queues, and backpressure prevent one dependency or tenant from exhausting shared resources.
4. **Consistency:** Deduplication, transactional outbox/inbox patterns, and reconciliation handle duplicates or uncertain outcomes. A DLQ is an operational holding area, not a substitute for recovery ownership.
5. **Degradation:** Return cached or reduced results only when their staleness and business impact are acceptable. Make the degraded state visible rather than silently losing important work.
6. **Evidence:** Emit metrics and traces for attempts, latency, saturation, breaker state, queue age, and final outcomes; test failure paths with controlled fault injection.
keyPoints: Derive deadlines from the end-to-end budget and propagate cancellation
Retry only transient, safely repeatable operations with a retry budget
Use circuit breakers, bulkheads, limits, and backpressure to contain failures
Design for duplicates and unknown outcomes with idempotency and reconciliation
Degrade explicitly and observe the final business outcome
Test failure paths under controlled conditions
redFlags: No timeout strategy ("we trust the network")
Retrying non-transient client failures or unknown writes without safe idempotency
No idempotency (retry creates duplicates)
Making an optional dependency fail the whole use case without a business reason

followUps.question: What is the difference between a timeout and a circuit breaker?
followUps.hint: Timeout: individual call protection. Circuit breaker: stops calling after repeated failures (protects the system, not just one call).

followUps.question: How do you test resilience patterns?
followUps.hint: Use contract tests plus controlled latency, error, disconnection, saturation, and duplicate-delivery injection. Verify business outcomes, resource recovery, alerts, and operator procedures.

realExperience.title: SGN3 resilience for external integrations
realExperience.description: SGN3 separates selected external synchronization from the enrollment transaction through durable messaging. Configured client timeouts bound synchronous integrations, while broker redelivery and a DLQ make failed asynchronous work visible for recovery. In an interview, the strongest version of this example should state which integrations actually use each policy and how final outcomes are monitored.

question: How do you modernise a legacy monolith incrementally?
shortAnswer: I modernize against a measurable outcome, not simply toward newer technology. First I characterize current behavior and create observability; then I find a seam, move one thin capability behind a stable contract, shift traffic or callers gradually, compare results, and remove the old path only after evidence supports it. Strangler Fig and branch by abstraction are useful techniques, while a rewrite remains a contextual option with a much larger proof burden.
detailedAnswer:
A modernization plan begins with the problem: unsupported runtime, release risk, security exposure, high change cost, capacity, or a required business capability. I baseline that outcome and map critical flows, dependencies, data ownership, and rollback constraints. Characterization tests and production telemetry reduce the risk of preserving unknown behavior.

For each slice I choose a seam—an HTTP route, message contract, module API, or abstraction around an implementation. Strangler Fig can redirect selected traffic to a new path; branch by abstraction can run old and new implementations behind one contract. Feature flags, shadow reads, reconciliation, and canary rollout help compare behavior before cutover.

Data deserves an explicit migration strategy: temporary shared access, backfill plus change capture, dual reads, or event-fed projections each have consistency and rollback trade-offs. Dual writes without a recovery design are especially risky. Every slice needs acceptance metrics, ownership, observability, compatibility rules, and a deletion condition so the program does not create a permanent second system.

A full replacement can be justified when incremental seams are impractical or the existing platform blocks the required outcome, but it should still prove scope, behavioral parity, data migration, cutover, and operational readiness. The choice is economic and technical, not a slogan.
keyPoints: Strangler Fig: new alongside old, migrate incrementally
Tie modernization to measurable business or operational outcomes
Make slices independently verifiable and reversible where practical
Plan data ownership, migration, reconciliation, and rollback explicitly
Define compatibility and deletion criteria to avoid a permanent dual system
redFlags: Proposing a rewrite without outcome, migration, or cutover evidence
Choosing an all-at-once cutover without proving why coexistence is impractical
Ignoring data migration complexity
Breaking active consumers unintentionally during a coexistence period

followUps.question: When can a replacement be justified?
followUps.hint: Compare incremental seams with replacement economics and required outcomes. Characterization, migration, cutover, and recovery evidence reduce risk; no single checklist item makes a rewrite correct.

followUps.question: How do you handle the database during strangler migration?
followUps.hint: Hardest part. Options: shared DB initially, CDC for sync, or database-per-service with eventual consistency.

realExperience.title: SGN3 incremental modernization
realExperience.description: The defensible SGN3 example is the selective extraction of integration responsibilities into senai-services while the core remained operational. Version upgrades can support the same incremental story, but their exact before/after versions and current status should be confirmed before the interview. Frame the result through reduced risk or lifecycle independence, not through the pattern name alone.

question: What is an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) and why use it?
shortAnswer: An ADR is a small, versioned record of one architecturally significant decision. It captures the context and forces, the chosen option, status, alternatives, and expected consequences. It preserves reasoning that code and diagrams rarely reveal, so a team can revisit the decision when assumptions change instead of treating it as timeless policy.
detailedAnswer:
A practical ADR has a clear title and status—for example proposed, accepted, deprecated, or superseded—plus the context and forces, the decision, credible alternatives, and positive and negative consequences. It records what the team knew at the time rather than trying to prove the choice was universally correct.

For example, a decision to move selected external synchronization to durable messaging should identify the user-facing latency and dependency failures that motivated it, compare synchronous calls, in-process work, and brokered delivery, and record consequences such as eventual consistency, duplicate handling, broker operations, and recovery ownership. Saying only 'use JMS' is a technology note, not sufficient decision context.

ADRs should live close to the system and be linked from relevant documentation or changes. When assumptions change, preserve the old record and add a superseding ADR; do not silently rewrite history. A small template and review habit matter more than a large document. Notes in a ticket or commit can retain useful context, but they are harder to discover and govern than a deliberate decision log.
keyPoints: Captures WHY, not just WHAT (code shows what)
Context + Decision + Alternatives + Consequences
Supports informed review when constraints or evidence change
New team members understand reasoning
Lightweight is better than no documentation
redFlags: No architectural documentation at all
Documentation that only shows the final state (no reasoning)
"The code is the documentation" for architectural decisions
Cannot explain why a decision was made

followUps.question: How do you keep ADRs up to date?
followUps.hint: Mark as superseded when decisions change. New ADR references the old one. Git history preserves the timeline.

followUps.question: What other architecture documentation do you use?
followUps.hint: C4 model (context, containers, components, code). Sequence diagrams for complex flows. Deployment diagrams.

realExperience.title: SGN3 decision documentation via feature analysis
realExperience.description: Feature-analysis documents that record constraints, decisions, and trade-offs exercise the same reasoning skill, but they should not be presented as formal ADR adoption if status, alternatives, and supersession are not maintained. Formal study supplies the ADR structure; the production evidence is transferable decision documentation.

question: When would you choose a synchronous API versus asynchronous messaging or events?
shortAnswer: I choose from the interaction's semantics. A synchronous API fits when the caller needs an immediate decision or current result and can tolerate temporal coupling. Messaging fits when work may complete later or needs buffering, fan-out, or independent recovery. The trade is not simply speed: asynchronous flows require explicit delivery guarantees, idempotency, ordering scope, observability, and a way to communicate final business outcomes.
detailedAnswer:
I first ask what the caller must know before it can proceed. A synchronous request is usually clearest for reads, validation, or commands that need an immediate authoritative outcome. It is easy to follow, but it couples availability and latency budgets across the call chain; deadlines, overload protection, and failure semantics must be designed.

A queue or command message is useful when work can be accepted and completed later, when bursts need buffering, or when producer and consumer should recover independently. An event is a fact that has already happened and can support fan-out or projections. It should not be disguised as a remote command. At-least-once delivery is common, so consumers need idempotency and duplicate-safe side effects; ordering is normally guaranteed only within a defined partition or key.

A common hybrid accepts a command synchronously, commits the state and an outbox record atomically, publishes an event asynchronously, and exposes status through polling, a callback, or another event. That creates eventual consistency, so pending and failed states, reconciliation, schema compatibility, correlation, and ownership must be visible.

I would document the API or event contract around business meaning, not database rows, and test consumer compatibility before rollout. The best choice is the simplest interaction that satisfies the required latency, consistency, availability, and recovery behavior.
keyPoints: Choose from required outcome, latency, consistency, and coupling
Distinguish commands that request work from events that report facts
Design delivery, idempotency, ordering scope, and recovery explicitly
Use an outbox when state change and publication must be coordinated
Expose pending, failed, and reconciled business states
redFlags: Using events only because they seem more scalable
Calling an imperative message an event to obscure ownership
Assuming exactly-once business effects without defining the boundary
No plan for schema evolution, duplicates, or failed consumers

followUps.question: How would you evolve an event without breaking consumers?
followUps.hint: Prefer compatible additions, stable semantics, contract tests, consumer inventory, and an explicit migration or versioning path for breaking changes.

followUps.question: How does the transactional outbox help, and what does it not solve?
followUps.hint: It atomically records state and publication intent. Relaying can still duplicate messages, so consumers and operational recovery remain necessary.

clarifyingQuestions: Does the caller require an immediate business outcome?
What ordering scope is actually required?
How will users and operators see pending or failed work?

realExperience.title: Synchronous core flow with durable integration work
realExperience.description: The SGN3 example can contrast immediate enrollment rules with selected external synchronization sent through durable messaging. The interview answer should name which state is committed first, how duplicate delivery is handled, how a failed synchronization is surfaced, and who owns reconciliation instead of stopping at 'we used JMS'.

question: How do you design data ownership and consistency across module or service boundaries?
shortAnswer: I assign one authoritative owner for each business fact and derive the consistency model from the invariants. Rules that must succeed atomically belong within one transaction boundary where possible. Other consumers use an API, event-fed projection, or governed read model and accept an explicit freshness target. A shared database or database-per-service can both work; ownership, access enforcement, migration, and recovery matter more than the slogan.
detailedAnswer:
I begin with business invariants: which facts must be checked and changed together, and what delay or temporary disagreement is acceptable? That identifies the transaction boundary more reliably than starting from tables or services. One module should be authoritative for each write model and publish a contract rather than expose unrestricted internal tables.

Within a boundary, a local ACID transaction is usually the simplest way to protect invariants. Across boundaries, options include synchronous orchestration, a saga with compensating actions, or asynchronous propagation into purpose-specific projections. These are not equivalent: a compensation is a new business action, not a database rollback, and eventual consistency requires visible pending states, idempotency, reconciliation, and operator ownership.

A shared database can be a pragmatic modular-monolith choice if write ownership, permissions, migrations, and cross-module queries are governed. Separate databases strengthen autonomy but add replication, joins, analytics, and distributed-workflow costs. Event publication commonly uses a transactional outbox; consumers track processed messages or make updates naturally idempotent.

Schema changes should follow an expand-migrate-contract sequence when old and new versions overlap. For reporting, I prefer a dedicated read model or warehouse when operational joins would defeat ownership or workload isolation. I validate the design with failure scenarios: duplicate events, delayed consumers, replay, partial migration, and reconciliation after an outage.
keyPoints: Derive transaction boundaries from business invariants
Give each business fact one authoritative write owner
Make freshness, pending states, and reconciliation explicit
Treat shared and separate databases as contextual trade-offs
Evolve schemas compatibly and test replay and recovery
redFlags: Multiple services updating the same tables without ownership
Calling compensation a guaranteed rollback
Claiming eventual consistency without a freshness or recovery plan
Dual writes to a database and broker with no atomicity strategy

followUps.question: When is a shared database acceptable?
followUps.hint: Discuss a single deployment or tightly coordinated context, explicit write ownership, permissions, migration coordination, and the autonomy intentionally traded away.

followUps.question: How would you reconcile a projection after missed or corrupt events?
followUps.hint: Use checkpoints, replayable sources or authoritative snapshots, idempotent rebuilds, comparison metrics, and a documented repair procedure.

realExperience.title: Shared-database ownership as a design trade-off
realExperience.description: SGN3's schema organization can illustrate ownership inside one operational database if the service-layer write paths and reporting exceptions are verified from the code you worked on. Present schemas as organizational support, describe the dependency controls you can demonstrate, and acknowledge that unrestricted cross-schema reads reduce isolation.
